candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
DreamCraft3D: Hierarchical 3D Generation with Bootstrapped Diffusion
  Prior
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
We present DreamCraft3D, a hierarchical 3D content generation method that
produces high-fidelity and coherent 3D objects. We tackle the problem by
leveraging a 2D reference image to guide the stages of geometry sculpting and
texture boosting. A central focus of this work is to address the consistency
issue that existing works encounter. To sculpt geometries that render
coherently, we perform score distillation sampling via a view-dependent
diffusion model. This 3D prior, alongside several training strategies,
prioritizes the geometry consistency but compromises the texture fidelity. We
further propose Bootstrapped Score Distillation to specifically boost the
texture. We train a personalized diffusion model, Dreambooth, on the augmented
renderings of the scene, imbuing it with 3D knowledge of the scene being
optimized. The score distillation from this 3D-aware diffusion prior provides
view-consistent guidance for the scene. Notably, through an alternating
optimization of the diffusion prior and 3D scene representation, we achieve
mutually reinforcing improvements: the optimized 3D scene aids in training the
scene-specific diffusion model, which offers increasingly view-consistent
guidance for 3D optimization. The optimization is thus bootstrapped and leads
to substantial texture boosting. With tailored 3D priors throughout the
hierarchical generation, DreamCraft3D generates coherent 3D objects with
photorealistic renderings, advancing the state-of-the-art in 3D content
generation. Code available at https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DreamCraft3D.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: DreamCraft3D introduces a hierarchical approach to 3D content generation that achieves high-fidelity and coherent 3D objects by leveraging 2D reference images. It addresses existing consistency issues in geometry and texture by employing a view-dependent diffusion model and Bootstrapped Score Distillation to enhance texture quality while preserving geometric integrity.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology of DreamCraft3D involves two primary stages: geometry sculpting and texture boosting. Geometry sculpting utilizes a score distillation sampling loss to ensure view consistency, alongside techniques like progressive view training and diffusion timestep annealing to refine the geometric details. Texture boosting is accomplished through Bootstrapped Score Distillation, which fine-tunes a diffusion model based on augmented multi-view renderings, enhancing texture realism while maintaining overall consistency.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup includes a benchmark test of 300 images which consist of real and generated samples along with corresponding depth maps and text prompts. The performance of DreamCraft3D was evaluated through quantitative metrics such as LPIPS, PSNR, and CLIP scores, as well as user studies for qualitative assessment. Comparisons were made against baseline methods like DreamFusion and Make-it-3D.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The limitations of DreamCraft3D include challenges with accurately capturing depth-related details, particularly in frontal-view geometries, and the inability to effectively separate material and lighting characteristics from the reference images. These issues may lead to artifacts in generated 3D models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions may involve enhancing the separation of material and lighting in generated 3D assets, improving strategies for depth accuracy, and exploring alternative frameworks for better handling more complex or diverse 3D structures.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents Purify++, a novel adversarial purification algorithm that enhances diffusion-based methods against adversarial attacks. It systematically improves diffusion purification through an improved diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal randomness control, achieving state-of-the-art performance on CIFAR10 against various adversarial attacks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: Purify++ employs an enhanced forward diffusion model based on variance-exploding diffusion mechanisms, utilizes advanced numerical solvers for purification (Heun's method), and optimally controls the randomness in the purification process through a dynamic mix coefficient, combining features of stochastic differential equations with ordinary differential equations.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted on benchmark datasets such as CIFAR10 and MNIST, using various neural network architectures (Wide ResNet, LeNet, and others). The performance was evaluated against multiple types of adversarial attacks, including PGD, SPSA, and Square Attack, measuring standard and robust accuracies to assess the effectiveness of the proposed method.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: Some limitations include potential computational costs related to high-order numerical solvers, reliance on the choice of hyperparameters for optimal performance, and the need for further exploration of randomness effects in diverse scenarios. Specific weaknesses related to testing against adaptive attacks or unseen adversarial strategies were not thoroughly discussed.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research may focus on exploring more sophisticated numerical solvers, extending the Purify++ framework to handle new forms of adversarial attacks, integrating additional preprocessing methods to enhance robustness, and conducting deeper theoretical analyses of the diffusion process within purification.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Disguised Copyright Infringement of Latent Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Copyright infringement may occur when a generative model produces samples
substantially similar to some copyrighted data that it had access to during the
training phase. The notion of access usually refers to including copyrighted
samples directly in the training dataset, which one may inspect to identify an
infringement. We argue that such visual auditing largely overlooks a concealed
copyright infringement, where one constructs a disguise that looks drastically
different from the copyrighted sample yet still induces the effect of training
Latent Diffusion Models on it. Such disguises only require indirect access to
the copyrighted material and cannot be visually distinguished, thus easily
circumventing the current auditing tools. In this paper, we provide a better
understanding of such disguised copyright infringement by uncovering the
disguises generation algorithm, the revelation of the disguises, and
importantly, how to detect them to augment the existing toolbox. Additionally,
we introduce a broader notion of acknowledgment for comprehending such indirect
access. Our code is available at
https://github.com/watml/disguised_copyright_infringement.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper challenges the traditional perspective of copyright infringement as it relates to generative models, specifically focusing on concealed copyright infringement that occurs through the use of disguised data. Their key contributions include the development of an algorithm to generate disguises, identification of detection methods for such disguises, and an expanded definition of 'access' to copyrighted materials in machine learning contexts.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The authors utilized Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) to develop an algorithm that generates disguised samples that are visually different from but structurally similar to copyrighted images. Key techniques include a feature similarity search for detecting potential privacy violations and an encoder-decoder framework for revealing hidden content in the disguised images.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments involved generating disguises from copyrighted images using varied base images, with different thresholds set for visual and feature distances. They evaluated the output using textual inversion and conducted experiments with LDMs and DreamBooth for fine-tuning. Datasets included images from known copyrighted works and generated disguises, with evaluations based on visual inspection and quantitative detection metrics.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper acknowledges that while their disguise generation techniques are effective, there is room for optimization. The effectiveness of disguises also heavily relies on the chosen base images and the parameters set during the disguise generation process. Additionally, it does not address the potential for 'chopping' copyrighted data across multiple images.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore quantifying how many disguises are required for reproducibility in large-scale training. Extensions may include optimization improvements for the existing algorithms and investigating the feasibility of creating 'smuggler’s datasets' that can effectively hide copyrighted data in multiple images.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
NaturalSpeech 3: Zero-Shot Speech Synthesis with Factorized Codec and
  Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
While recent large-scale text-to-speech (TTS) models have achieved
significant progress, they still fall short in speech quality, similarity, and
prosody. Considering speech intricately encompasses various attributes (e.g.,
content, prosody, timbre, and acoustic details) that pose significant
challenges for generation, a natural idea is to factorize speech into
individual subspaces representing different attributes and generate them
individually. Motivated by it, we propose NaturalSpeech 3, a TTS system with
novel factorized diffusion models to generate natural speech in a zero-shot
way. Specifically, 1) we design a neural codec with factorized vector
quantization (FVQ) to disentangle speech waveform into subspaces of content,
prosody, timbre, and acoustic details; 2) we propose a factorized diffusion
model to generate attributes in each subspace following its corresponding
prompt. With this factorization design, NaturalSpeech 3 can effectively and
efficiently model intricate speech with disentangled subspaces in a
divide-and-conquer way. Experiments show that NaturalSpeech 3 outperforms the
state-of-the-art TTS systems on quality, similarity, prosody, and
intelligibility, and achieves on-par quality with human recordings.
Furthermore, we achieve better performance by scaling to 1B parameters and 200K
hours of training data.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: NaturalSpeech 3 proposes a novel TTS system that uses factorized diffusion models for zero-shot speech synthesis, enhancing speech quality, similarity, and prosody by decomposing speech into distinct subspaces (content, prosody, timbre, acoustic details) and generating them separately.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The system employs a neural codec using factorized vector quantization (FVQ) for speech attribute disentanglement and a factorized diffusion model for generating speech attributes based on separate prompts, allowing effective modeling of complex speech information.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: NaturalSpeech 3 was trained on the Librilight dataset, containing 60K hours of speech data. Evaluations were conducted using LibriSpeech and RA VDESS benchmarks to measure performance on similarity, quality, and intelligibility. Objective metrics included SIM-O, SIM-R, WER, and subjective metrics included CMOS and SMOS.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The study is limited by its attribute coverage, specifically lacking features like background sounds. It's also restricted to the English language, hindering capabilities in multilingual TTS. The neural codec requires phoneme transcription for supervision, which limits scalability.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work will explore additional speech attributes, develop methods for better disentanglement without supervision, collect diverse multilingual datasets, and extend applications of FACodec to zero-shot voice conversion and automatic speech recognition.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
NExT-Mol: 3D Diffusion Meets 1D Language Modeling for 3D Molecule
  Generation
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
3D molecule generation is crucial for drug discovery and material design.
While prior efforts focus on 3D diffusion models for their benefits in modeling
continuous 3D conformers, they overlook the advantages of 1D SELFIES-based
Language Models (LMs), which can generate 100% valid molecules and leverage the
billion-scale 1D molecule datasets. To combine these advantages for 3D molecule
generation, we propose a foundation model -- NExT-Mol: 3D Diffusion Meets 1D
Language Modeling for 3D Molecule Generation. NExT-Mol uses an extensively
pretrained molecule LM for 1D molecule generation, and subsequently predicts
the generated molecule's 3D conformers with a 3D diffusion model. We enhance
NExT-Mol's performance by scaling up the LM's model size, refining the
diffusion neural architecture, and applying 1D to 3D transfer learning.
Notably, our 1D molecule LM significantly outperforms baselines in
distributional similarity while ensuring validity, and our 3D diffusion model
achieves leading performances in conformer prediction. Given these improvements
in 1D and 3D modeling, NExT-Mol achieves a 26% relative improvement in 3D FCD
for de novo 3D generation on GEOM-DRUGS, and a 13% average relative gain for
conditional 3D generation on QM9-2014. Our codes and pretrained checkpoints are
available at https://github.com/acharkq/NExT-Mol.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces NExT-Mol, a novel foundation model that integrates 1D SELFIES-based language modeling with 3D diffusion models for improved 3D molecule generation, achieving significant advancements in both de novo and conditional 3D molecular generation tasks, evidenced by substantial improvements in relevant performance metrics.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: NExT-Mol employs an autoregressive transformer language model (MoLlama) for generating 1D molecule sequences, followed by a Diffusion Molecular Transformer (DMT) to predict the corresponding 3D molecular conformers. The approach utilizes strategies such as scaling the LM size, refining the neural architecture for diffusion, and implementing transfer learning to enhance model performance between 1D and 3D tasks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The research evaluated NExT-Mol on standard datasets including GEOM-DRUGS and QM9-2014 for de novo 3D molecule generation, with performance benchmarks against both established baselines and metrics for 3D conformer prediction, using datasets with specific splits for comprehensive validation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The model struggles with generalization to unseen scaffolds and computational cost when incorporating large parameter models like MoLlama. There are also concerns regarding the quadratic memory complexity of DMT's pair representation, which might limit its scalability with larger molecular structures.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work includes exploring advanced generalization techniques for better performance on unseen scaffolds, implementing randomized SELFIES during pretraining, extending NExT-Mol for structure-based molecule generation, and investigating improved sampling methodologies like classifier guidance.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Accelerating Parallel Sampling of Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have emerged as state-of-the-art generative models for image
generation. However, sampling from diffusion models is usually time-consuming
due to the inherent autoregressive nature of their sampling process. In this
work, we propose a novel approach that accelerates the sampling of diffusion
models by parallelizing the autoregressive process. Specifically, we
reformulate the sampling process as solving a system of triangular nonlinear
equations through fixed-point iteration. With this innovative formulation, we
explore several systematic techniques to further reduce the iteration steps
required by the solving process. Applying these techniques, we introduce
ParaTAA, a universal and training-free parallel sampling algorithm that can
leverage extra computational and memory resources to increase the sampling
speed. Our experiments demonstrate that ParaTAA can decrease the inference
steps required by common sequential sampling algorithms such as DDIM and DDPM
by a factor of 4$\sim$14 times. Notably, when applying ParaTAA with 100 steps
DDIM for Stable Diffusion, a widely-used text-to-image diffusion model, it can
produce the same images as the sequential sampling in only 7 inference steps.
The code is available at https://github.com/TZW1998/ParaTAA-Diffusion.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper addresses the slow sampling process of diffusion models by proposing ParaTAA, a novel parallel sampling algorithm that accelerates the inference steps required for image generation while maintaining high image quality. It demonstrates a significant reduction in required sampling steps by a factor of 4 to 14 compared to sequential methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves reformulating the diffusion model sampling process as solving a system of triangular nonlinear equations using fixed-point iteration. The paper introduces several optimization techniques to improve the efficiency of the fixed-point iteration, including constructing better iteration functions and employing a variant of Anderson Acceleration tailored for triangular nonlinear equations.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments evaluate the performance of ParaTAA against sequential sampling algorithms like DDIM and DDPM, using standard datasets and metrics such as FID, IS, and CLIP Scores. The evaluation is done on popular diffusion models, DiT and Stable Diffusion, under various sampling step scenarios (e.g., 25, 50, 100 steps).
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The performance of ParaTAA is constrained by the need for multiple GPUs to achieve significant speedup in wall-clock time. The paper also notes that while the parallel approach is effective, it may not yet be applicable to higher-order solvers, leaving those extensions for future work.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore the application of ParaTAA to autoregressive processes in other generative models, including autoregressive video generative models. Additionally, theoretical aspects of the triangular Anderson acceleration technique may warrant further investigation.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Improving Diffusion Models for Inverse Problems Using Optimal Posterior
  Covariance
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Recent diffusion models provide a promising zero-shot solution to noisy
linear inverse problems without retraining for specific inverse problems. In
this paper, we reveal that recent methods can be uniformly interpreted as
employing a Gaussian approximation with hand-crafted isotropic covariance for
the intractable denoising posterior to approximate the conditional posterior
mean. Inspired by this finding, we propose to improve recent methods by using
more principled covariance determined by maximum likelihood estimation. To
achieve posterior covariance optimization without retraining, we provide
general plug-and-play solutions based on two approaches specifically designed
for leveraging pre-trained models with and without reverse covariance. We
further propose a scalable method for learning posterior covariance prediction
based on representation with orthonormal basis. Experimental results
demonstrate that the proposed methods significantly enhance reconstruction
performance without requiring hyperparameter tuning.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper addresses the improvement of diffusion models for noisy linear inverse problems by optimizing the posterior covariance using maximum likelihood estimation. The authors demonstrate that current methods employ isotropic Gaussian approximations for the conditional posterior mean and propose enhancements to existing models without the need for retraining and hyperparameter tuning, significantly improving reconstruction performance across various tasks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves optimizing posterior covariance through maximum likelihood estimation with two plug-and-play techniques, which include using available reverse covariance and employing Monte Carlo estimation for cases where reverse covariance is absent. Additionally, the authors introduce a scalable approach for learning posterior covariance using orthonormal basis representation to reduce complexity.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments are conducted on two datasets: FFHQ and ImageNet for various inverse tasks such as inpainting, deblurring (Gaussian and motion), and super-resolution. The models are benchmarked using metrics such as SSIM, LPIPS, and FID, with a unified codebase to ensure fair comparison of performance across different implemented methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The proposed method relies on the diagonal assumption for covariance, which can limit the optimality of results even in well-trained models. Additionally, there are challenges in accurately predicting posterior covariance in high-dimensional data scenarios, leading to possible approximation errors in certain conditions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could focus on developing advanced principles for covariance design, exploring nonlinear transformations for better correlation reductions, and investigating efficient approximation methods for moment matching to enhance performance in high-dimensional scenarios.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Bridging discrete and continuous state spaces: Exploring the Ehrenfest
  process in time-continuous diffusion models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative modeling via stochastic processes has led to remarkable empirical
results as well as to recent advances in their theoretical understanding. In
principle, both space and time of the processes can be discrete or continuous.
In this work, we study time-continuous Markov jump processes on discrete state
spaces and investigate their correspondence to state-continuous diffusion
processes given by SDEs. In particular, we revisit the $\textit{Ehrenfest
process}$, which converges to an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process in the infinite
state space limit. Likewise, we can show that the time-reversal of the
Ehrenfest process converges to the time-reversed Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process.
This observation bridges discrete and continuous state spaces and allows to
carry over methods from one to the respective other setting. Additionally, we
suggest an algorithm for training the time-reversal of Markov jump processes
which relies on conditional expectations and can thus be directly related to
denoising score matching. We demonstrate our methods in multiple convincing
numerical experiments.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper investigates the relationship between time-continuous Markov jump processes on discrete state spaces and state-continuous diffusion processes represented by stochastic differential equations (SDEs). It introduces the Ehrenfest process as a bridge between these two frameworks and proposes a new loss function for training Markov jump processes, highlighting the correspondence to score-based generative modeling.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The authors derive a loss function based on conditional expectations for training discrete diffusion models, which allows linking jump moments of the Ehrenfest process to score functions in continuous state settings. The Ehrenfest process is treated as the discrete analog of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, with time-reversal properties investigated to relate jump processes to score-based generative modeling.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Multiple numerical experiments are conducted using the Ehrenfest process, including a tractable toy example, MNIST dataset for image modeling, and CIFAR-10 dataset for more complex scenarios. The experiments compare different variants of the proposed loss functions, specifically focusing on performance metrics such as Inception Score (IS) and Frechet Inception Distance (FID).
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper does not explicitly mention limitations in the model or methodology, but some potential limitations could include the dependency on the size of the discrete state space and the challenge of approximating transition probabilities accurately.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore generalizations of the time-reversal convergence results, further tuning of the proposed loss functions, and methods for improving performance in complex generative modeling scenarios, as well as potential applications in transfer learning between discrete and continuous state spaces.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper proposes the first exact framework for training diffusion models from corrupted data, using a double application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function. It addresses memorization issues in diffusion models and shows that models trained on corrupted data replicate the training dataset significantly less than those trained on clean data while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: A method that involves Ambient Denoising Score Matching for noise levels greater than observed and a consistency loss function to facilitate learning optimal denoisers for lower noise levels. It employs a framework to fine-tune the Stable Diffusion XL model using only noisy samples.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilize a random subset of 10,000 images from the LAION dataset, applying significant corruption through masking and noise addition. Various noise levels and models are compared, including evaluation of denoising performance and FID scores for unconditional image generation, with some models fine-tuned on different noise levels.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The approach does not address the training of diffusion models with linearly corrupted data and requires increased training time when using the consistency loss. Preliminary trials on very limited datasets (< 100 samples) indicated failures in the proposed objective's performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Exploration of further applications of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data, optimization of efficiency in training with consistency loss, and testing on even smaller datasets to refine the methods.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Accelerating Convergence of Score-Based Diffusion Models, Provably
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Score-based diffusion models, while achieving remarkable empirical
performance, often suffer from low sampling speed, due to extensive function
evaluations needed during the sampling phase. Despite a flurry of recent
activities towards speeding up diffusion generative modeling in practice,
theoretical underpinnings for acceleration techniques remain severely limited.
In this paper, we design novel training-free algorithms to accelerate popular
deterministic (i.e., DDIM) and stochastic (i.e., DDPM) samplers. Our
accelerated deterministic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/{T}^2)$ with $T$ the
number of steps, improving upon the $O(1/T)$ rate for the DDIM sampler; and our
accelerated stochastic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/T)$, outperforming the
rate $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ for the DDPM sampler. The design of our algorithms
leverages insights from higher-order approximation, and shares similar
intuitions as popular high-order ODE solvers like the DPM-Solver-2. Our theory
accommodates $\ell_2$-accurate score estimates, and does not require
log-concavity or smoothness on the target distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper addresses the problem of low sampling speed in score-based diffusion models by proposing novel training-free algorithms that accelerate both deterministic (DDIM) and stochastic (DDPM) samplers. The main contributions include an accelerated deterministic sampler with a convergence rate of O(1/T^2) and an accelerated stochastic sampler with a convergence rate of O(1/T), outperforming existing models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The proposed methods leverage higher-order approximation insights, allowing the deterministic sampler to utilize a momentum term for updates and the stochastic sampler to incorporate noise in an optimized manner. Theoretical guarantees for convergence rates are established for both samplers under general assumptions about score accuracy.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup involves benchmarking the performance of the proposed samplers against DDIM and DDPM using datasets like CelebA-HQ, LSUN-Bedroom, and LSUN-Churches. The same pre-trained score functions are used across all samplers during evaluation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The study assumes ℓ2-accurate score estimates and does not impose stringent conditions on the target distribution, but the results might not hold for ℓ∞ score estimation accuracy. The convergence analysis remains sub-optimal regarding the problem dimension dependency.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could focus on refining the theoretical framework to improve dimension dependency, extending the acceleration techniques to higher-order ODE solvers, and developing improved algorithms for SDE-based sampling methods.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
The Emergence of Reproducibility and Generalizability in Diffusion
  Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
In this work, we investigate an intriguing and prevalent phenomenon of
diffusion models which we term as "consistent model reproducibility": given the
same starting noise input and a deterministic sampler, different diffusion
models often yield remarkably similar outputs. We confirm this phenomenon
through comprehensive experiments, implying that different diffusion models
consistently reach the same data distribution and scoring function regardless
of diffusion model frameworks, model architectures, or training procedures.
More strikingly, our further investigation implies that diffusion models are
learning distinct distributions affected by the training data size. This is
supported by the fact that the model reproducibility manifests in two distinct
training regimes: (i) "memorization regime", where the diffusion model overfits
to the training data distribution, and (ii) "generalization regime", where the
model learns the underlying data distribution. Our study also finds that this
valuable property generalizes to many variants of diffusion models, including
those for conditional use, solving inverse problems, and model fine-tuning.
Finally, our work raises numerous intriguing theoretical questions for future
investigation and highlights practical implications regarding training
efficiency, model privacy, and the controlled generation of diffusion models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper presents a comprehensive investigation of reproducibility in diffusion models, demonstrating that different diffusion architectures learn almost identical mappings to the same data distribution, emphasizing the phenomenon of 'consistent model reproducibility' under varying training conditions and sizes.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The authors employ denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPM), consistency models, and various architectures on the CIFAR-10 dataset to analyze how reproducibility manifests in two training regimes: the memorization regime and the generalization regime. They introduce quantitative metrics such as reproducibility and generalization scores to evaluate their findings.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments are conducted using several diffusion models trained on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The reproducibility scores are calculated based on the similarity of images generated from the same noise input across different models. They employ deterministic samplers to ensure consistent evaluation, comparing models like DDPM, EDM, U-ViT, and others under different training settings.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The study primarily focuses on the CIFAR-10 dataset, which may limit the generalizability of findings to other datasets. Furthermore, reproducibility is noted primarily within similar architectures, indicating potential limitations when comparing diffusion models of differing architectures or those applied in out-of-distribution scenarios.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore improving training efficiencies based on reproducibility insights, investigate privacy concerns related to black-box diffusion models, and develop methods for controlling data generation processes effectively using the unique mappings learned by diffusion models.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
NaturalSpeech 3: Zero-Shot Speech Synthesis with Factorized Codec and
  Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
While recent large-scale text-to-speech (TTS) models have achieved
significant progress, they still fall short in speech quality, similarity, and
prosody. Considering speech intricately encompasses various attributes (e.g.,
content, prosody, timbre, and acoustic details) that pose significant
challenges for generation, a natural idea is to factorize speech into
individual subspaces representing different attributes and generate them
individually. Motivated by it, we propose NaturalSpeech 3, a TTS system with
novel factorized diffusion models to generate natural speech in a zero-shot
way. Specifically, 1) we design a neural codec with factorized vector
quantization (FVQ) to disentangle speech waveform into subspaces of content,
prosody, timbre, and acoustic details; 2) we propose a factorized diffusion
model to generate attributes in each subspace following its corresponding
prompt. With this factorization design, NaturalSpeech 3 can effectively and
efficiently model intricate speech with disentangled subspaces in a
divide-and-conquer way. Experiments show that NaturalSpeech 3 outperforms the
state-of-the-art TTS systems on quality, similarity, prosody, and
intelligibility, and achieves on-par quality with human recordings.
Furthermore, we achieve better performance by scaling to 1B parameters and 200K
hours of training data.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research introduces NaturalSpeech 3, a TTS system utilizing factorized diffusion models for zero-shot speech synthesis, achieving superior speech quality, similarity, and prosody by decomposing speech into distinct attributes such as content, prosody, timbre, and acoustic details.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves a novel neural codec with factorized vector quantization (FVQ) to disentangle speech attributes and a factorized diffusion model that generates speech attributes based on specific prompts.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments use datasets including Librilight with 60K hours of unlabeled speech data and 7000 distinct speakers. The evaluation benchmarks include LibriSpeech for overall performance and RA VDESS for prosody evaluation, employing metrics like CMOS, SMOS, WER, and MCD.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: Limitations include the attribute coverage not encompassing all speech aspects (e.g., background sounds) and a reliance on English corpus which restricts multilingual capabilities, with FACodec requiring phoneme transcription for supervision.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research may focus on expanding attribute coverage to include energy and background sounds, enhancing multilingual support, exploring unsupervised methods for disentanglement, and applying FACodec to other tasks such as automatic speech recognition.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
DreamCraft3D: Hierarchical 3D Generation with Bootstrapped Diffusion
  Prior
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
We present DreamCraft3D, a hierarchical 3D content generation method that
produces high-fidelity and coherent 3D objects. We tackle the problem by
leveraging a 2D reference image to guide the stages of geometry sculpting and
texture boosting. A central focus of this work is to address the consistency
issue that existing works encounter. To sculpt geometries that render
coherently, we perform score distillation sampling via a view-dependent
diffusion model. This 3D prior, alongside several training strategies,
prioritizes the geometry consistency but compromises the texture fidelity. We
further propose Bootstrapped Score Distillation to specifically boost the
texture. We train a personalized diffusion model, Dreambooth, on the augmented
renderings of the scene, imbuing it with 3D knowledge of the scene being
optimized. The score distillation from this 3D-aware diffusion prior provides
view-consistent guidance for the scene. Notably, through an alternating
optimization of the diffusion prior and 3D scene representation, we achieve
mutually reinforcing improvements: the optimized 3D scene aids in training the
scene-specific diffusion model, which offers increasingly view-consistent
guidance for 3D optimization. The optimization is thus bootstrapped and leads
to substantial texture boosting. With tailored 3D priors throughout the
hierarchical generation, DreamCraft3D generates coherent 3D objects with
photorealistic renderings, advancing the state-of-the-art in 3D content
generation. Code available at https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DreamCraft3D.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: DreamCraft3D introduces a hierarchical approach to 3D content generation that achieves high-fidelity and coherent 3D objects by leveraging 2D reference images. It addresses existing consistency issues in geometry and texture by employing a view-dependent diffusion model and Bootstrapped Score Distillation to enhance texture quality while preserving geometric integrity.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The methodology of DreamCraft3D involves two primary stages: geometry sculpting and texture boosting. Geometry sculpting utilizes a score distillation sampling loss to ensure view consistency, alongside techniques like progressive view training and diffusion timestep annealing to refine the geometric details. Texture boosting is accomplished through Bootstrapped Score Distillation, which fine-tunes a diffusion model based on augmented multi-view renderings, enhancing texture realism while maintaining overall consistency.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup includes a benchmark test of 300 images which consist of real and generated samples along with corresponding depth maps and text prompts. The performance of DreamCraft3D was evaluated through quantitative metrics such as LPIPS, PSNR, and CLIP scores, as well as user studies for qualitative assessment. Comparisons were made against baseline methods like DreamFusion and Make-it-3D.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The limitations of DreamCraft3D include challenges with accurately capturing depth-related details, particularly in frontal-view geometries, and the inability to effectively separate material and lighting characteristics from the reference images. These issues may lead to artifacts in generated 3D models.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions may involve enhancing the separation of material and lighting in generated 3D assets, improving strategies for depth accuracy, and exploring alternative frameworks for better handling more complex or diverse 3D structures.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title: Disguised Copyright Infringement of Latent Diffusion Models
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Copyright infringement may occur when a generative model produces samples
substantially similar to some copyrighted data that it had access to during the
training phase. The notion of access usually refers to including copyrighted
samples directly in the training dataset, which one may inspect to identify an
infringement. We argue that such visual auditing largely overlooks a concealed
copyright infringement, where one constructs a disguise that looks drastically
different from the copyrighted sample yet still induces the effect of training
Latent Diffusion Models on it. Such disguises only require indirect access to
the copyrighted material and cannot be visually distinguished, thus easily
circumventing the current auditing tools. In this paper, we provide a better
understanding of such disguised copyright infringement by uncovering the
disguises generation algorithm, the revelation of the disguises, and
importantly, how to detect them to augment the existing toolbox. Additionally,
we introduce a broader notion of acknowledgment for comprehending such indirect
access. Our code is available at
https://github.com/watml/disguised_copyright_infringement.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: This paper challenges the traditional perspective of copyright infringement as it relates to generative models, specifically focusing on concealed copyright infringement that occurs through the use of disguised data. Their key contributions include the development of an algorithm to generate disguises, identification of detection methods for such disguises, and an expanded definition of 'access' to copyrighted materials in machine learning contexts.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The authors utilized Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) to develop an algorithm that generates disguised samples that are visually different from but structurally similar to copyrighted images. Key techniques include a feature similarity search for detecting potential privacy violations and an encoder-decoder framework for revealing hidden content in the disguised images.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments involved generating disguises from copyrighted images using varied base images, with different thresholds set for visual and feature distances. They evaluated the output using textual inversion and conducted experiments with LDMs and DreamBooth for fine-tuning. Datasets included images from known copyrighted works and generated disguises, with evaluations based on visual inspection and quantitative detection metrics.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The paper acknowledges that while their disguise generation techniques are effective, there is room for optimization. The effectiveness of disguises also heavily relies on the chosen base images and the parameters set during the disguise generation process. Additionally, it does not address the potential for 'chopping' copyrighted data across multiple images.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore quantifying how many disguises are required for reproducibility in large-scale training. Extensions may include optimization improvements for the existing algorithms and investigating the feasibility of creating 'smuggler’s datasets' that can effectively hide copyrighted data in multiple images.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
NaturalSpeech 3: Zero-Shot Speech Synthesis with Factorized Codec and
  Diffusion Models
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
While recent large-scale text-to-speech (TTS) models have achieved

prosody. Considering speech intricately encompasses various attributes (e.g.,

Furthermore, we achieve better performance by scaling to 1B parameters and 200K

selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The system employs a neural codec using factorized vector quantization (FVQ) for speech attribute disentanglement and a factorized diffusion model for generating speech attributes based on separate prompts, allowing effective modeling of complex speech information.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: NaturalSpeech 3 was trained on the Librilight dataset, containing 60K hours of speech data. selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The study is limited by its attribute coverage, specifically lacking features like background sounds. It's also restricted to the English language, hindering capabilities in multilingual TTS. The neural codec requires phoneme transcription for supervision, which limits scalability.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work will explore additional speech attributes, develop methods for better disentanglement without supervision, collect diverse multilingual datasets, and extend applications of FACodec to zero-shot voice conversion and automatic speech recognition.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper proposes the first exact framework for training diffusion models from corrupted data, using a double application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function. It addresses memorization issues in diffusion models and shows that models trained on corrupted data replicate the training dataset significantly less than those trained on clean data while maintaining competitive performance.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: A method that involves Ambient Denoising Score Matching for noise levels greater than observed and a consistency loss function to facilitate learning optimal denoisers for lower noise levels. It employs a framework to fine-tune the Stable Diffusion XL model using only noisy samples.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilize a random subset of 10,000 images from the LAION dataset, applying significant corruption through masking and noise addition. Various noise levels and models are compared, including evaluation of denoising performance and FID scores for unconditional image generation, with some models fine-tuned on different noise levels.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The approach does not address the training of diffusion models with linearly corrupted data and requires increased training time when using the consistency loss. Preliminary trials on very limited datasets (< 100 samples) indicated failures in the proposed objective's performance.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Exploration of further applications of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data, optimization of efficiency in training with consistency loss, and testing on even smaller datasets to refine the methods.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title: Accelerating Convergence of Score-Based Diffusion Models, Provably
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Score-based diffusion models, while achieving remarkable empirical
performance, often suffer from low sampling speed, due to extensive function
evaluations needed during the sampling phase. Despite a flurry of recent
activities towards speeding up diffusion generative modeling in practice,
theoretical underpinnings for acceleration techniques remain severely limited.
In this paper, we design novel training-free algorithms to accelerate popular
deterministic (i.e., DDIM) and stochastic (i.e., DDPM) samplers. Our
accelerated deterministic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/{T}^2)$ with $T$ the
number of steps, improving upon the $O(1/T)$ rate for the DDIM sampler; and our
accelerated stochastic sampler converges at a rate $O(1/T)$, outperforming the
rate $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ for the DDPM sampler. The design of our algorithms
leverages insights from higher-order approximation, and shares similar
intuitions as popular high-order ODE solvers like the DPM-Solver-2. Our theory
accommodates $\ell_2$-accurate score estimates, and does not require
log-concavity or smoothness on the target distribution.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper addresses the problem of low sampling speed in score-based diffusion models by proposing novel training-free algorithms that accelerate both deterministic (DDIM) and stochastic (DDPM) samplers. The main contributions include an accelerated deterministic sampler with a convergence rate of O(1/T^2) and an accelerated stochastic sampler with a convergence rate of O(1/T), outperforming existing models.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The proposed methods leverage higher-order approximation insights, allowing the deterministic sampler to utilize a momentum term for updates and the stochastic sampler to incorporate noise in an optimized manner. Theoretical guarantees for convergence rates are established for both samplers under general assumptions about score accuracy.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup involves benchmarking the performance of the proposed samplers against DDIM and DDPM using datasets like CelebA-HQ, LSUN-Bedroom, and LSUN-Churches. The same pre-trained score functions are used across all samplers during evaluation.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The study assumes ℓ2-accurate score estimates and does not impose stringent conditions on the target distribution, but the results might not hold for ℓ∞ score estimation accuracy. The convergence analysis remains sub-optimal regarding the problem dimension dependency.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could focus on refining the theoretical framework to improve dimension dependency, extending the acceleration techniques to higher-order ODE solvers, and developing improved algorithms for SDE-based sampling methods.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
NaturalSpeech 3: Zero-Shot Speech Synthesis with Factorized Codec and
  Diffusion Models
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
While recent large-scale text-to-speech (TTS) models have achieved
significant progress, they still fall short in speech quality, similarity, and
prosody. Considering speech intricately encompasses various attributes (e.g.,
content, prosody, timbre, and acoustic details) that pose significant
challenges for generation, a natural idea is to factorize speech into
individual subspaces representing different attributes and generate them
individually. Motivated by it, we propose NaturalSpeech 3, a TTS system with
novel factorized diffusion models to generate natural speech in a zero-shot
way. Specifically, 1) we design a neural codec with factorized vector
quantization (FVQ) to disentangle speech waveform into subspaces of content,
prosody, timbre, and acoustic details; 2) we propose a factorized diffusion
model to generate attributes in each subspace following its corresponding
prompt. With this factorization design, NaturalSpeech 3 can effectively and
efficiently model intricate speech with disentangled subspaces in a
divide-and-conquer way. Experiments show that NaturalSpeech 3 outperforms the
state-of-the-art TTS systems on quality, similarity, prosody, and
intelligibility, and achieves on-par quality with human recordings.
Furthermore, we achieve better performance by scaling to 1B parameters and 200K
hours of training data.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The research introduces NaturalSpeech 3, a TTS system utilizing factorized diffusion models for zero-shot speech synthesis, achieving superior speech quality, similarity, and prosody by decomposing speech into distinct attributes such as content, prosody, timbre, and acoustic details.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves a novel neural codec with factorized vector quantization (FVQ) to disentangle speech attributes and a factorized diffusion model that generates speech attributes based on specific prompts.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments use datasets including Librilight with 60K hours of unlabeled speech data and 7000 distinct speakers. The evaluation benchmarks include LibriSpeech for overall performance and RA VDESS for prosody evaluation, employing metrics like CMOS, SMOS, WER, and MCD.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: Limitations include the attribute coverage not encompassing all speech aspects (e.g., background sounds) and a reliance on English corpus which restricts multilingual capabilities, with FACodec requiring phoneme transcription for supervision.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research may focus on expanding attribute coverage to include energy and background sounds, enhancing multilingual support, exploring unsupervised methods for disentanglement, and applying FACodec to other tasks such as automatic speech recognition.

{"arxiv_id":"2407.11451v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2407.11451v1","title":"Isometric Representation Learning for Disentangled Latent Space of
  Diffusion Models","authors":["Jaehoon Hahm","Junho Lee","Sunghyun Kim","Joonseok Lee"],"published_date":"2024-07-16T07:36:01Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"The latent space of diffusion model mostly still remains unexplored, despite
its great success and potential in the field of generative modeling. In fact,
the latent space of existing diffusion models are entangled, with a distorted
mapping from its latent space to image space. To tackle this problem, we
present Isometric Diffusion, equipping a diffusion model with a geometric
regularizer to guide the model to learn a geometrically sound latent space of
the training data manifold. This approach allows diffusion models to learn a
more disentangled latent space, which enables smoother interpolation, more
accurate inversion, and more precise control over attributes directly in the
latent space. Our extensive experiments consisting of image interpolations,
image inversions, and linear editing show the effectiveness of our method.","github_url":"https://github.com/isno0907/isodiff","main_contributions":"The paper addresses the challenge of entangled latent spaces in diffusion models by introducing Isometric Diffusion, which incorporates geometric regularization to learn a disentangled latent space, enabling smoother interpolation, accurate inversion, and fine control over attributes.","methodology":"The authors proposed Isometric Diffusion, which uses an isometric representation learning regularizer to encourage the mapping from latent space to image space to preserve distances and angles, effectively improving the geometric alignment of the latent space.","experimental_setup":"Experiments were conducted using benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, LSUN-Church, and LSUN-Bedrooms. Evaluation metrics included Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), Perceptual Path Length (PPL), Linear Separability (LS), Mean Condition Number (MCN), and a novel metric, Mean Relative Trajectory Length (mRTL).","limitations":"The paper highlights potential trade-offs between image generation quality and disentanglement metrics, indicating that the effectiveness of the proposed method may vary based on specific applications and user needs. The computational load for cost-effective training and inference is also noted.","future_research_directions":"Future research could focus on applying the proposed method to conditional generation, exploring ways to minimize the trade-off between disentanglement and image quality, and examining the effectiveness of the method in large-scale training data settings."}
add_method_texts:
{"arxiv_id":"2310.16818v2","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2310.16818v2","title":"DreamCraft3D: Hierarchical 3D Generation with Bootstrapped Diffusion
  Prior","authors":["Jingxiang Sun","Bo Zhang","Ruizhi Shao","Lizhen Wang","Wen Liu","Zhenda Xie","Yebin Liu"],"published_date":"2023-10-25T17:50:10Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"We present DreamCraft3D, a hierarchical 3D content generation method that
produces high-fidelity and coherent 3D objects. We tackle the problem by
leveraging a 2D reference image to guide the stages of geometry sculpting and
texture boosting. A central focus of this work is to address the consistency
issue that existing works encounter. To sculpt geometries that render
coherently, we perform score distillation sampling via a view-dependent
diffusion model. This 3D prior, alongside several training strategies,
prioritizes the geometry consistency but compromises the texture fidelity. We
further propose Bootstrapped Score Distillation to specifically boost the
texture. We train a personalized diffusion model, Dreambooth, on the augmented
renderings of the scene, imbuing it with 3D knowledge of the scene being
optimized. The score distillation from this 3D-aware diffusion prior provides
view-consistent guidance for the scene. Notably, through an alternating
optimization of the diffusion prior and 3D scene representation, we achieve
mutually reinforcing improvements: the optimized 3D scene aids in training the
scene-specific diffusion model, which offers increasingly view-consistent
guidance for 3D optimization. The optimization is thus bootstrapped and leads
to substantial texture boosting. With tailored 3D priors throughout the
hierarchical generation, DreamCraft3D generates coherent 3D objects with
photorealistic renderings, advancing the state-of-the-art in 3D content
generation. Code available at https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DreamCraft3D.","github_url":"https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DreamCraft3D","main_contributions":"DreamCraft3D introduces a hierarchical approach to 3D content generation that achieves high-fidelity and coherent 3D objects by leveraging 2D reference images. It addresses existing consistency issues in geometry and texture by employing a view-dependent diffusion model and Bootstrapped Score Distillation to enhance texture quality while preserving geometric integrity.","methodology":"The methodology of DreamCraft3D involves two primary stages: geometry sculpting and texture boosting. Geometry sculpting utilizes a score distillation sampling loss to ensure view consistency, alongside techniques like progressive view training and diffusion timestep annealing to refine the geometric details. Texture boosting is accomplished through Bootstrapped Score Distillation, which fine-tunes a diffusion model based on augmented multi-view renderings, enhancing texture realism while maintaining overall consistency.","experimental_setup":"The experimental setup includes a benchmark test of 300 images which consist of real and generated samples along with corresponding depth maps and text prompts. The performance of DreamCraft3D was evaluated through quantitative metrics such as LPIPS, PSNR, and CLIP scores, as well as user studies for qualitative assessment. Comparisons were made against baseline methods like DreamFusion and Make-it-3D.","limitations":"The limitations of DreamCraft3D include challenges with accurately capturing depth-related details, particularly in frontal-view geometries, and the inability to effectively separate material and lighting characteristics from the reference images. These issues may lead to artifacts in generated 3D models.","future_research_directions":"Future research directions may involve enhancing the separation of material and lighting in generated 3D assets, improving strategies for depth accuracy, and exploring alternative frameworks for better handling more complex or diverse 3D structures."}

{"arxiv_id":"2404.06737v4","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2404.06737v4","title":"Disguised Copyright Infringement of Latent Diffusion Models","authors":["Yiwei Lu","Matthew Y. R. Yang","Zuoqiu Liu","Gautam Kamath","Yaoliang Yu"],"published_date":"2024-04-10T04:55:57Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Copyright infringement may occur when a generative model produces samples
substantially similar to some copyrighted data that it had access to during the
training phase. The notion of access usually refers to including copyrighted
samples directly in the training dataset, which one may inspect to identify an
infringement. We argue that such visual auditing largely overlooks a concealed
copyright infringement, where one constructs a disguise that looks drastically
different from the copyrighted sample yet still induces the effect of training
Latent Diffusion Models on it. Such disguises only require indirect access to
the copyrighted material and cannot be visually distinguished, thus easily
circumventing the current auditing tools. In this paper, we provide a better
understanding of such disguised copyright infringement by uncovering the
disguises generation algorithm, the revelation of the disguises, and
importantly, how to detect them to augment the existing toolbox. Additionally,
we introduce a broader notion of acknowledgment for comprehending such indirect
access. Our code is available at
https://github.com/watml/disguised_copyright_infringement.","github_url":"https://github.com/watml/disguised_copyright_infringement","main_contributions":"This paper challenges the traditional perspective of copyright infringement as it relates to generative models, specifically focusing on concealed copyright infringement that occurs through the use of disguised data. Their key contributions include the development of an algorithm to generate disguises, identification of detection methods for such disguises, and an expanded definition of 'access' to copyrighted materials in machine learning contexts.","methodology":"The authors utilized Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) to develop an algorithm that generates disguised samples that are visually different from but structurally similar to copyrighted images. Key techniques include a feature similarity search for detecting potential privacy violations and an encoder-decoder framework for revealing hidden content in the disguised images.","experimental_setup":"Experiments involved generating disguises from copyrighted images using varied base images, with different thresholds set for visual and feature distances. They evaluated the output using textual inversion and conducted experiments with LDMs and DreamBooth for fine-tuning. Datasets included images from known copyrighted works and generated disguises, with evaluations based on visual inspection and quantitative detection metrics.","limitations":"The paper acknowledges that while their disguise generation techniques are effective, there is room for optimization. The effectiveness of disguises also heavily relies on the chosen base images and the parameters set during the disguise generation process. Additionally, it does not address the potential for 'chopping' copyrighted data across multiple images.","future_research_directions":"Future work could explore quantifying how many disguises are required for reproducibility in large-scale training. Extensions may include optimization improvements for the existing algorithms and investigating the feasibility of creating 'smuggler’s datasets' that can effectively hide copyrighted data in multiple images."}

new_method:
Below is the outcome of step 3—a novel method that builds on the Base Method and incorporates ideas from DreamCraft3D to tackle several issues identified in the original approach.

Proposed Method: Hierarchically Factorized Isometric Diffusion (HFID)

Overview:
HFID introduces a two-stage, hierarchical latent space framework that both preserves global geometric consistency (as in the Base Method) and decouples local/textural information (inspired by the hierarchical consistency in DreamCraft3D). This new approach addresses two prominent issues with the Base Method:
 • The inherent trade-off between disentanglement (via isometric regularization) and high-quality image generation.
 • The high computational load from enforcing global geometric consistency on a single, monolithic latent space.

Method Description:
1. Stage One – Global Structure Encoding:
 • A coarse latent representation is learned using an isometric diffusion model very similar to the Base Method, where a geometric regularizer enforces distance–angle preservation.
 • This stage is responsible for capturing the global structure and overall semantic layout of the training data. In doing so, it creates a “global latent code” that is robust and disentangled.
 • The key innovation here is to set up the training so that the isometric regularization is applied only to this coarse latent layer. Consequently, there is less pressure on the higher-resolution details, mitigating the trade-off between disentanglement and image fidelity.

2. Stage Two – Local Detail Refinement (Texture & Fine Attributes):
 • In a second, parallel diffusion process, one focuses on learning a fine, residual latent space that captures local details (e.g., texture, lighting, and nuanced features).
 • This stage is inspired by DreamCraft3D’s hierarchical approach, wherein a “bootstrapped” or auxiliary diffusion model refines outputs by utilizing the coarse latent code as a structural guide.
 • A dedicated consistency loss is introduced between the fine-level outputs and their corresponding reconstructions from the global stage. This encourages the fine-scale model to inherit the global geometric structure while being free to optimize local realism and texture fidelity.
 • By alternating the training between the global (isometric) stage and the local refinement stage, the system learns how to balance both aspects: maintaining strong global disentanglement while also producing high-fidelity, photorealistic outputs.

Key Advantages:
• Decoupling global structural features from local details allows each diffusion process to be optimized for its specific role. This improves the generation quality without forcing a single model to handle competing objectives.
• The hierarchical design reduces computational load by reusing the coarse latent space as a scaffold. The detailed diffusion process only models residuals, offering efficiency gains.
• The method enables more precise local control in downstream applications (e.g., targeted editing or inversion) by leveraging the disentangled global space for broad attributes and the fine latent space for intricate details.

In summary, Hierarchically Factorized Isometric Diffusion (HFID) extends the Base Method by introducing a two-tier latent space architecture:
 – Stage one enforces global disentanglement via isometric regularization,
 – Stage two refines local textures and details using hierarchical loss and bootstrapped score distillation inspired by DreamCraft3D.
This approach addresses the trade-offs in image quality versus disentanglement and improves computational efficiency, offering a genuinely novel pathway for enhanced control and output fidelity in diffusion models.

dataset.details: The primary dataset used is CelebA-HQ, which is a high-quality face dataset. Additional datasets include CIFAR-10, LSUN-Church, and LSUN-Bedrooms.

machine_learning_model.code:
def isometry_loss_h(f, x_samples, timesteps, args, device):
    bs = x_samples.shape[0]
    u = torch.randn_like(x_samples, device=device)
    Ju = A.jvp(lambda z: f(z, timesteps, return_dict=True)[1], x_samples, u)[1]
    JTJu = A.vjp(lambda z: f(z, timesteps, return_dict=True)[1], x_samples, Ju)[1]
    TrR = torch.sum((Ju.view(bs, -1) ** 2), dim=1).mean()
    TrR2 = torch.sum(JTJu.view(bs, -1) ** 2, dim=1).mean()
    isometry_losses = TrR2 / TrR ** 2
    iso_loss = isometry_losses
    return iso_loss
machine_learning_model.details: The implementation uses a modified UNet architecture with isometric regularization for diffusion models. The core innovation is the addition of geometric regularizers that guide the model to learn a geometrically sound latent space.
